notes.section_title: 4.1 How is a Major Policy Decision Taken?

topics.topic_name: The Mandal Commission and the Reservation Policy Decision

content.text: Understanding how government works requires seeing how major decisions are actually made. The decision to reserve 27% government jobs for Socially and Educationally Backward Classes (SEBC) in 1990 illustrates how multiple institutions and political processes shape policy.

The Government of India issued Office Memorandum No. 36012/31/90-Est on August 13, 1990, announcing the reservation policy. This simple-looking government order was the culmination of a long chain of events: In 1979, the Mandal Commission was appointed to identify backward classes. In 1980, it submitted its report recommending 27% job reservation. For several years, parliamentarians and parties debated the recommendations. In the 1989 elections, the Janata Dal promised implementation; when it won and formed government, V.P. Singh became Prime Minister and announced the policy.

content.title: Decision-Making Chain
content.text: This case shows decision-making involves: (1) Commission studying the issue; (2) Parliament discussing recommendations; (3) Political parties debating in election manifestos; (4) Election results; (5) Cabinet decision; (6) Official announcement through government order; (7) Implementation by bureaucracy; (8) Court challenges and judiciary deciding constitutionality.

The decision was highly controversial. Newspapers debated for months. Some felt reservations were necessary to end caste discrimination; others felt they violated merit and equal opportunity. After months of protest and counter-protest, the Supreme Court reviewed the policy. In 1992, the Court upheld reservations as constitutional but directed the government to exclude well-to-do backward class members, ensuring reservations benefited the truly disadvantaged.

topics.topic_name: Understanding Decision-Making: Roles of Key Institutions

content.title: Who Makes Major Decisions?
items: The President of India is the formal head of state and highest formal authority
The Prime Minister is the head of government and exercises all governmental powers
The Cabinet of Ministers (top party leaders) takes the most important decisions in Cabinet meetings
Parliament passes laws and scrutinizes government decisions
Civil servants/bureaucrats implement decisions through government machinery
The Supreme Court can review decisions and declare them invalid if unconstitutional

content.text: The reservation decision illustrates that no single person or institution decides alone. The Prime Minister was crucial but couldn't have implemented the policy without Cabinet support, parliamentary support, bureaucratic cooperation, and ultimate acceptance by courts. Governing requires coordination among multiple institutions.

Democracy requires that those who make decisions be accountable to people. Parliament is the mechanism for this accountability. In every democracy, an assembly of elected representatives exercises supreme political authority on behalf of the people. In India, this is called Parliament.

content.title: Four Key Functions of Parliament
items: Parliament is the final authority for making laws—it can make new laws, change existing laws, or abolish them
Parliament exercises control over those who run the government; in India, government can take decisions only with Parliament's support
Parliament controls all money governments have; public money can be spent only when Parliament sanctions it
Parliament is the highest forum for discussion and debate on public issues; it can seek information about any matter

content.title: Parliamentary Supremacy
content.text: Parliament's control over government is particularly strong in India. The Prime Minister and all ministers must maintain the confidence of the majority in Lok Sabha (lower house). If Parliament passes a 'no confidence' motion, the government must resign. This makes government accountable to elected representatives who are accountable to voters.

topics.topic_name: Two Houses of Parliament: Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha

content.text: Indian Parliament has two houses. The Lok Sabha (House of the People) is directly elected by people; the Rajya Sabha (Council of States) is elected indirectly by state legislatures. This bicameral structure allows division of labor and provides additional checks.

content.title: Lok Sabha Features
items: 543 members elected directly by people for five-year terms
Elected through universal adult franchise from constituencies
More powerful in most matters
Controls government—Prime Minister must have Lok Sabha majority
Can be dissolved before five years if government loses majority

content.title: Rajya Sabha Features
items: 250 members; 238 elected by state legislatures, 12 nominated by President
Members serve six-year terms; one-third retire every two years
Represents states and ensures their interests are considered
Cannot reject money bills; can only delay them by 14 days
Not dissolved; permanent body

content.title: Relationship Between Houses
content.text: For ordinary laws, if the two houses disagree, the matter is resolved in a joint session where members of both houses meet together. Since Lok Sabha has more members, its position typically prevails. For money bills (laws about taxation and spending), the Lok Sabha's position is final—Rajya Sabha cannot reject, only delay. This reflects democratic principle that directly elected body controlling money is more powerful.

topics.topic_name: How Parliament Works: A Day in Lok Sabha

content.text: When Parliament is in session, various activities occur daily. Ministers answer questions from MPs. Bills are introduced and debated. Resolutions are passed. Reports are presented. Parliamentary committees examine issues in detail. This work makes government accountable and allows people's voices (through elected representatives) to influence governance.

content.title: Question Hour
content.text: Each day of Lok Sabha session begins with Question Hour when MPs can ask ministers about government policies and decisions. This is crucial accountability mechanism—ministers must publicly explain and defend their actions. Opposition can point out failures; media reports answers; public learns what government is doing. Question Hour creates daily pressure on ministers to remain responsive.

Bills are debated extensively. A bill goes through three readings in Parliament before becoming law. In each reading, members can raise objections, suggest amendments, and debate implications. This lengthy process ensures laws are carefully considered. Committees of Parliament also examine bills in detail, suggesting improvements before final passage.

The executive is divided into two categories. The political executive consists of elected representatives (Prime Minister and ministers) who take policy decisions. The permanent executive (civil servants) implements these decisions. This separation ensures that day-to-day administration continues even as governments change.

content.title: Why Political Executive Must Be More Powerful
content.text: While civil servants may have more technical expertise and job security, ministers have final decision-making power. Why? Because in democracy, elected representatives are accountable to people. The minister, being an elected representative, can be voted out if voters disapprove. Civil servants are not elected and cannot be held electorally accountable. Therefore, ultimate authority rests with elected ministers, even when they lack technical expertise. They take advice from experts but make final decisions.

topics.topic_name: Prime Minister and Council of Ministers

content.text: The Prime Minister is the head of government and the most powerful executive official. The Prime Minister is appointed by the President but must have support of Lok Sabha majority. Once appointed, the PM appoints other ministers from among elected members of Parliament.

content.title: Powers of the Prime Minister
items: Chairs Cabinet meetings and makes decisions in Cabinet
Coordinates work of different government departments
Has final say in case of disagreement between departments
Exercises general supervision over all ministries
Distributes and redistributes work among ministers
Has power to dismiss ministers
When PM resigns, the entire ministry resigns

content.title: Cabinet System of Government
content.text: The Cabinet includes about 25 senior ministers. Though all government work is done in the name of PM and ministers, Cabinet functions as a team. Ministers may have different opinions, but once Cabinet decides, all must publicly support the decision. Even if a minister disagrees, they cannot openly criticize Cabinet decisions. This collective responsibility ensures government speaks with one voice.

The PM is the most important political institution in India. Over decades, the PM's power has concentrated as political parties have become centralized around top leaders. Media coverage focuses on PM more than Parliament. This concentration of power has made parliamentary democracy in some countries appear more like 'Prime Ministerial government' than Cabinet government. However, rising coalition governments (where multiple parties form government together) have somewhat checked PM power by requiring accommodation of coalition partners.

The judiciary is essential for democracy. All courts at different levels (Supreme Court, High Courts, District Courts) together form the judiciary. The independent judiciary ensures that government power is checked and constitutional rights are protected.

content.title: What If Courts Were Not Independent?
content.text: In the Mandal Commission case, if courts were not independent, the government's decision could not have been reviewed. A dependent judiciary would have simply approved government action. Independent courts allowed both supporters and opponents of reservations to present their cases, and judges decided on constitutional grounds, not political pressure. This independence is essential for protecting people from government abuse.

content.title: Key Features Ensuring Judicial Independence
items: Judges are appointed through constitutional procedures, not political nomination alone
Senior judges select new judges; political executive has limited influence
Once appointed, judges cannot be arbitrarily removed; removal requires two-thirds vote of both Parliament houses (impeachment)
Judges have security of tenure—cannot be transferred against their will
Judges receive salaries directly from government treasury; cannot be starved financially

content.text: Indian courts have wide powers. The Supreme Court and High Courts can hear cases between: citizens; citizens and government; different state governments; Union and state governments. They are the highest courts of appeal. More importantly, they can examine if any government action or law violates the Constitution.

content.title: Judicial Powers
items: Courts can interpret the Constitution and determine its meaning
Courts can declare any law or government action invalid if it violates the Constitution (judicial review)
Courts can issue writs directing government to take or stop taking action
Courts can protect fundamental rights of citizens
Courts can hear cases involving public interest (Public Interest Litigation)
Courts can issue directions to protect environment, ensure fair trial, protect workers' rights

content.title: Judicial Review
content.text: The power of courts to examine and potentially invalidate laws and actions is called judicial review. This is crucial check on government and legislature. Even if Parliament passes a law, if courts find it violates constitutional rights or spirit, they can strike it down. Courts have used this power to expand rights, protect minorities, and keep government accountable.

Indian courts developed an innovative practice allowing anyone to approach courts on behalf of public interest, not just individuals directly affected. This opens courts to people's movements and civil society groups fighting for justice on issues like environmental protection, worker rights, and social justice.

content.title: Significance of PIL
content.text: Through PIL, courts have heard cases on: right to environment (environmental pollution), right to food and shelter, rights of street children, bonded labor, manual scavenging. This allows courts to proactively protect rights and direct government to fulfill constitutional promises. It expands democratic participation beyond voting—people can demand justice directly from courts.

topics.topic_name: Role of Civil Servants in Implementation

content.text: While political leaders (ministers) make policy decisions, civil servants implement them. The civil service consists of permanent officials appointed through competitive examination. They work in various departments and are responsible for day-to-day administration. Police officers, tax collectors, doctors in government hospitals, teachers in government schools—all are civil servants.

content.title: Permanent Executive
content.text: Civil servants are called permanent executive because they remain in office even when governments change. When a government is voted out, civil servants continue serving the new government. This continuity ensures that basic administration continues despite electoral changes. However, civil servants must implement the policies of the elected government, not their own preferences.

The most senior civil servants are called 'secretaries' in various ministries. They provide technical advice and expertise to ministers. A secretary might know more about their ministry's subject than the minister, but the minister has final decision-making authority. The secretary implements ministerial decisions even if they disagree with them. This ensures elected leadership, not bureaucratic control.

Democratic institutions involve rules and procedures that bind leaders' hands. One person cannot make all decisions; decisions must go through committees and multiple approvals. This is frustrating and slow, but necessary. Without institutions, even well-meaning leaders could become tyrannical.

content.title: Why Institutions Matter
content.text: Some might argue it would be better if one person made all decisions without procedures and committees. However, this ignores a crucial insight: institutional delays and complexity protect against tyranny. When decision-making is easy and concentrated, it is also easy for rulers to abuse power. Institutions ensure that: (1) No single person has unlimited power; (2) Multiple perspectives are considered; (3) Minorities are protected; (4) Decisions are made transparently and can be scrutinized.

Democracy works well when institutions perform their designated functions. Parliament legislates and controls government; Executive implements laws; Judiciary protects rights. When institutions overlap or one dominates others, democracy weakens. This requires constant vigilance by citizens to ensure institutions function properly and independently.

glossary.definition: A system of procedures, rules, and structures for making and implementing decisions. In democracy, key institutions are Parliament, Executive, and Judiciary, each with defined powers and accountabilities.
The branch of government responsible for implementing laws and policies. Divided into political executive (elected ministers) and permanent executive (civil servants).
The head of government and most powerful executive official. Appointed by President but must have Lok Sabha majority support; appoints other ministers and has final decision-making authority.
The council of senior ministers headed by Prime Minister; meets to make important policy decisions. Functions as a team with collective responsibility—all members must publicly support decisions.
The supreme legislative body consisting of elected representatives. Has two houses (Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha) and is the final authority for making and changing laws.
The lower house of Parliament, directly elected by people; 543 members serve five-year terms. More powerful than Rajya Sabha in most matters; government must have Lok Sabha majority.
The upper house of Parliament, elected indirectly by state legislatures; 250 members with six-year staggered terms. Represents state interests; cannot reject money bills.
An official announcement or decision issued by government through appropriate ministry, implementing policy decisions previously made.
Senior ministers who head major ministries and meet as Cabinet to take most important policy decisions.
A parliamentary motion voting on whether government retains Parliament's confidence. If passed, government must resign.
The system of courts and judges responsible for interpreting laws, protecting rights, and checking government actions for constitutionality.
The power of courts to examine whether laws or government actions violate the Constitution and declare them invalid if they do.
The highest court in the country, capable of hearing appeals from lower courts and reviewing constitutionality of any law or government action.
A formal order issued by courts directing government officials to act or stop acting in a particular way; used to enforce rights.
Legal mechanism allowing any citizen or organization to approach courts on behalf of public interest, not just individual interest.
The permanent bureaucracy consisting of appointed officials responsible for day-to-day administration and implementation of government decisions.
A government official or administrator in the civil service; appointed through competitive examination rather than elected.
The supreme law of the country containing fundamental rules governing state structure, powers, and citizen rights that all other laws must follow.
The requirement that rulers must answer to people for their actions; ensured through elections, parliamentary scrutiny, and media oversight.
The division of governmental authority among Parliament (legislative), Executive (administrative), and Judiciary (judicial) branches to prevent concentration of power.

summary_points: Major policy decisions in democracy involve multiple institutions working together. The 1990 reservation policy illustrates: Commission studies issue, Parliament debates, parties include in manifestos, elections occur, Cabinet decides, order is issued, bureaucrats implement, courts review constitutionality. No single institution alone makes such decisions.

Parliament is supreme in democracy, exercising four key functions: making/changing laws, controlling government through no-confidence motions, controlling public money, and serving as forum for public debate. Government can take decisions only with Parliament's support.

India's bicameral Parliament divides labor between Lok Sabha (directly elected, more powerful, controls government) and Rajya Sabha (indirectly elected, represents states). For ordinary laws, both houses must agree; for money bills, Lok Sabha's position is final, reflecting that elected body controlling public money is most powerful.

The Prime Minister is the head of government and most powerful executive official, appointed by President but requiring Lok Sabha majority. PM distributes work among ministers, chairs Cabinet, and has final say in disagreements. Cabinet functions as team with collective responsibility, but power has increasingly concentrated in PM's hands.

Political executive (elected ministers) makes policy decisions; permanent executive (civil servants) implements them. Though civil servants may have more expertise, ministers have final authority because they are electorally accountable. Civil servants continue serving despite electoral changes, ensuring administrative continuity.

Independent judiciary ensures government power is checked and constitutional rights protected. Through judicial review, courts can declare laws or actions invalid if unconstitutional. Judges appointed through constitutional procedures and cannot be arbitrarily removed, protecting their independence from government pressure.

Through Public Interest Litigation, courts can hear cases on behalf of public interest, allowing citizens and organizations to approach courts for justice on environmental protection, worker rights, and social issues. This expands democracy beyond voting.

Democratic institutions involve rules and procedures that slow decision-making but protect against tyranny. Institutions ensure multiple perspectives are considered, minorities are protected, and decisions are transparent and scrutinizable. Though frustrating, this complexity is essential for democracy.

Parliament, Executive, and Judiciary must each perform their designated functions independently. When institutions overlap or one dominates, democracy weakens. Citizens must ensure institutions function properly through vigilance and participation.

The Mandal Commission case shows democracy's institutional check on power: government decided policy, but independent courts reviewed it, heard both supporters and opponents, and modified implementation to prevent injustice. This demonstrates why institutional independence matters—it prevents any single center of power from being absolute.
